flashcards.Back: The specific point where the aircraft would balance if suspended. It is the theoretical point where the entire weight of the sUAS is concentrated.
Stability about the lateral axis of the aircraft. It is primarily influenced by the location of the Center of Gravity.
A balance point located toward the front of the aircraft. It increases longitudinal stability but makes the aircraft nose-heavy and harder to flare.
A balance point located toward the rear of the aircraft. It decreases stability and makes the sUAS highly susceptible to uncontrollable pitch or stalls.
Loading an aircraft with a rearward balance point creates extreme instability. This condition can lead to difficulties in recovering from stalls or spins.
The highest allowable weight for the sUAS to begin flight. It must be less than $55$ lbs for operations under Part $107$.
Small unmanned aircraft systems must weigh less than $55$ lbs. This weight includes everything on board or attached to the aircraft.
Exceeding weight limits significantly reduces the rate of climb. The extra mass requires more lift and power than the propulsion system can provide.
An aircraft carrying excessive weight requires a higher speed to generate enough lift. This results in a longer takeoff or launch distance.
Operating above the maximum weight can cause structural failure during maneuvers. The airframe may not support the increased load factors during flight.
The Remote Pilot-in-Command is legally responsible for ensuring the sUAS is properly loaded. This check must be performed before every flight.
The primary source for determining the correct loading of an sUAS. Pilots should follow the specific weight and balance charts provided by the manufacturer.
The standard weight of the aircraft excluding payload. It typically includes the airframe, motors, and fixed internal components.
The weight of items added to the aircraft that are not essential for flight. Common examples include cameras, sensors, and delivery packages.
Pressure altitude corrected for nonstandard temperature. It is a measure of the thickness or density of the air where the aircraft is operating.
Performance is significantly reduced because the air is thinner. This condition leads to less lift from wings and less thrust from propellers.
Increasing temperature causes air to expand and become less dense. Higher temperatures result in a higher density altitude and lower aircraft performance.
Air becomes thinner at higher altitudes due to lower atmospheric pressure. High-elevation launch sites naturally experience high density altitude conditions.
Moist air is less dense than dry air because water vapor is lighter than air molecules. High humidity increases density altitude and reduces performance.
Aerodynamic surfaces generate less lift when the air is less dense. This requires the aircraft to move faster or use higher power to maintain flight.
Propellers are less efficient in thin air because they have fewer air molecules to push. This results in reduced total thrust for the sUAS.
Power output decreases at high density altitudes because there is less air for cooling. Efficiency drops as the propulsion system works harder in thin air.
Higher density altitude requires a longer takeoff run. The sUAS must achieve a higher groundspeed to generate sufficient lift in thin air.
The true airspeed at which an aircraft stalls increases in thin air. Pilots must be aware of the reduced safety margins during maneuvers.
A value of $29.92$ inches of mercury ($Hg$) at sea level. Decreases in pressure lead to higher density altitude and reduced performance.
A temperature of $15^\circ$ Celsius ($59^\circ$ Fahrenheit) at sea level. Temperatures above this value decrease aircraft performance by increasing density altitude.
The ratio of the total air load on the wings to the actual weight of the aircraft. It is expressed in units of $G$ (gravitational force).
An aircraft in straight-and-level, unaccelerated flight maintains a load factor of $1$ $G$. The lift produced equals the aircraft weight.
As the angle of bank increases in a level turn, the load factor increases exponentially. This makes the aircraft feel significantly heavier to the airframe.
In a $60^\circ$ coordinated bank turn, the load factor is $2$ $G$s. The aircraft structure must support twice its actual weight.
An $80^\circ$ bank turn results in a load factor of $5.76$ $G$s. Most sUAS structures cannot withstand this level of stress without failing.
Stall speed increases as the square root of the load factor. This means an aircraft will stall at a higher speed during steep turns.
A stall that occurs at a higher-than-normal airspeed due to increased load factor. This often happens during abrupt maneuvers or steep banks.
Maneuvering in steep banks dramatically increases the stall speed. Pilots must maintain higher airspeeds to avoid an aerodynamic stall during the turn.
Resistance to rolling, which is influenced by weight distribution. Stability about this axis ensures the sUAS returns to level flight after a disturbance.
A condition caused by an extreme aft Center of Gravity. The aircraft becomes tail-heavy, making it impossible for the pilot to lower the nose.
The ability of an aircraft to achieve specific results like climb, speed, and endurance. It is directly affected by weight and air density.
High air density (low density altitude) improves performance. Thicker air provides more lift, more thrust, and better engine cooling for the sUAS.
Launching at high altitudes reduces sUAS performance due to thinner air. Pilots should expect slower climbs and longer takeoff distances than at sea level.
Hot weather raises density altitude, leading to sluggish flight characteristics. Batteries may also overheat more quickly in these conditions.
To maintain altitude in a turn, the total lift must increase to support the weight and centrifugal force. This increase in lift raises the load factor.
Every sUAS has a limit to how many $G$s it can safely handle. Exceeding these limits can cause permanent airframe deformation or structural breakage.

flashcards.Front: Landing Speed at High Density Altitude
flashcards.Back: The sUAS will have a higher true airspeed on approach. This results in a faster groundspeed and a longer landing roll.
Heavier aircraft are less agile and have higher maneuvering speeds. The increased inertia makes it harder to change direction or stop a climb.
The range of balance points within which the aircraft can be safely operated. Flying outside these limits makes the sUAS unstable or uncontrollable.
Using larger batteries increases flight time but adds significant weight. This can negatively impact the power-to-weight ratio and overall maneuverability.
Adding external cameras or sensors can shift the Center of Gravity. This shift may cause the aircraft to tilt or drift uncontrollably during flight.
In a coordinated turn, the load factor results from centrifugal force and gravity. Steeper turns require more lift, which increases the $G$-load on the aircraft.
The process of checking that the payload and batteries are positioned correctly. This ensures the aircraft remains stable and controllable throughout the flight.
Water vapor in the air reduces the number of air molecules available for lift. This makes the air thinner and reduces the efficiency of the rotors.
